sentences.text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]
Good morning.
Certainly.
At the time of my birth, my name was Zora Hollanderova or Hollender.
H-O-L-L-E-N-D-E-R.
My birth date is January 20, 1930.
My age today is 67, and I live in San Francisco, California, USA.
I was born in Czechoslovakia.
In a small town, in the suburb of Beregszasz.
I was born in Nagy Bereg-- N-A-G-Y B-E-R-E-G.
Beregszasz is B-E-R-E-G-S-Z-A with an umlaut, S-Z.
We lived only 8 kilometers from-- from the city, so--
My dad, and my mother, and grandma lived with us-- or we lived with them-- in a seven-room house.
And we had a big barn with cattle, horses, cows, and we had chickens, ducks, turkeys, geese-- animals you find on the farm.
And we had cats, and we had our dog.
And we had a well on our property, which was unusual in our area.
Later on, we had indoor plumbing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My dad and mother owned fields-- grape fields, a vineyard.
And so dad was a winemaker.
And we had 24 pieces of land.
We had a threshing machine, which went from farm to farm.
It was rented out when we didn't use it.
And it-- we all-- it-- it provided us with additional income.
This was in partnership with two other non-Jewish partners and friends of my father's.
Mother and dad also owned a-- a store.
He was-- he was a [HUNGARIAN]-- that's a Hungarian word for someone who runs a variety store.
Mother and grandma, really, ran the store, and dad and mother would shop on every Wednesday in Beregszasz or Berehove, which was its Czech name before 1938.
Unfortunately, in '39 we had to close our store down, and we had to depend on the farm for sustenance.
My dad's name was David Hollender-- H-O-L-L-E-N-D-E-R.
And mother's name was Helene, with an umlaut over the last E, maiden name Gelb, our last name, Hollender.
In the-- in the-- around the town, in the periphery of the town, of Nagy Bereg-- so that horses could pull the wagons for harvest.
And we actually had migrant workers help us every summer.
These were White Russians who came into town and helped us with the planting in the spring and-- or late fall, and then with the harvest, whenever things ripened and was ready to be harvested.
Oh, sometimes I went along, but I was a student, and I was busy studying.
And also, actually each of us-- my four brothers and my younger sister-- there were six of us all together originally-- we all had jobs to do on the farm.

The-- the men children took care of the cattle-- brushed them down, feed them, and changed the hay and helped milk-- milk the cows-- milking the cows was really mother's duty-- and collecting the eggs in the baskets.
It was great fun.
And-- I-- I really had the job of bringing the wood in that was readied for the winter.
Would ?] bring the wood indoors, because the stoves, for-- for a number of years, were-- we-- we burned wood.
They were wood stoves.
You bet.
My oldest brother was Joseph Hollender, and then came Michael.
Joseph's nickname was Jozsi, and Michael's nickname was Miksa, and it still is.
Then came Sandor, and his nickname was Sanyi.
Then came Victor, and he didn't have a nickname.
And I was the fifth child after four boys.
And my nickname was Hanci from birth-- my grandchildren call me Grandma Hanci, which I love.
My younger sister was Annuska-- her formal name is Anna.
Oh, yes.
Joseph, Jozsi's nickname was Yechoshuah.
Then Michael's name was Mosche.
Sandor's Hebrew name was Simcha.
And Victor is Benjamin Wolf and mine is Channah, and my sister's Hebrew name is Shifra.
Yes, we had one synagogue.
And it was very crowded in it because even though we had only about 100 families, it-- we all had large families.
And-- but most of them were Hollenders.
[LAUGHS] So we-- we filled up the synagogue with Hollenders.
I-- I remember very warmly.
I don't remember the name of the synagogue, but we didn't have the three-- the three branches of Judaism that we have in the United States.
It was Orthodox.
So far as I know, all the synagogues were Orthodox in my region.
Oh, we celebrated all the holidays with the family together, and dinner, and the traditional foods.
The menfolk went to the synagogue, and-- and the women went also when they could, but mainly on the holidays.
But they had to stay home and prepare the food for the rest of the family, when they returned home from the synagogue.
Yes, my brothers put on Tefillin in the morning.
They had to get up very early, and they took care of the animals, had breakfast, and then they went off to school or to work later.
But it was-- it seems like a hard life, however, it-- it wasn't.
It-- we had a very warm and lovely family.
I think my brothers were so handsome.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We sang a lot in our family.
Oh, I remember lots of them.
In fact, after I survived the Holocaust, I had the need to write it-- write down the songs that my brothers had taught me and my sister.
I still remember-- whichever brother it was, for whichever particular song-- they put their arms around us, and they would teach us these songs from the city.
Oh, yes, there were all sorts of songs.
Oh, folk songs, mostly.
They have to do with birds and love and the moon.
Very romantic, in fact, as I-- as I think of it, today.
Oh, I-- I could rattle off quite a few.
[LAUGHS] Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I will take too long to-- to do this.
I'm not quite prepared for this, but all right.
[SINGING IN HUNGARIAN]
That was Hungarian.
In fact, recently when we were in Mexico, they were playing some of the songs I learned in Hungarian.
The same melody, and I found out that it originated from Mexico, and some of them in South America.
Originally, it was Czech.
We children spoke Czech.

However, I was only eight years old when this eastern part of Czechoslovakia, called Ruthenia in English, was taken away from Czechoslovakia, and it became a part of Hungary under the Munich Agreement.
And so we became Hungary from then on.
And of course, that's when our troubles started.
I was transferred from Czech school to Hungarian schools-- school, which was mandatory for us.
I also spoke Hungarian, but better Czech.
In school, we always spoke Czech, and the teachers spoke Czech, and our homework was in Czech.
And I started learning my three R's in Czech.
So from the age of eight, it became Hungarian, and it was like starting from zero, because I didn't know how to write in Hungarian until then.
So between the ages of eight and 14, I spoke Hungarian.
And that is the language that I still speak fluently-- I read it and I write it.
How did your parents cope with the change in language?
Not quite as well as children, but as I remember, they also started phasing out.

It was much easier for them to speak Hungarian, because they were part of the Austro-Hungarian empire, and they spoke Hungarian prior to the area becoming Czechoslovakia in World War I, when the Austro-Hungarian empire broke up.
Traditionally.
We-- Mother lit the Shabbat candles, and we said the bracha-- the prayer-- and then my dad would say [NON-ENGLISH], and we had traditional foods-- wonderful foods.
All right, we started out usually with fish, and we had chicken and farfel, and salad-- which consisted of, not the tossed salad that we have here, but cut up vegetables.
And we would have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chicken soup, of course, which I forgot.
[LAUGHS] And-- and some sort of compote for dessert.
And wine, of course-- wine went with every meal, including breakfast.
[LAUGHS] In the wintertime, we would make mulled wine, and before I went off to school, I-- I would have a glass of mulled wine.
Nobody was drunk in my family, ever.
And we-- we just enjoyed wine-- red wine, particularly, at the time.
That's a good question.
I-- I went to a public school.
Elementary school was all public in our area, and we were excused during the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] it's-- it was called, which is religious hour.
And I think it was a couple of hours, or an hour and a half.
I'm not sure exactly.
But we were excused and-- because we were Jewish, and my parents wanted us to learn Hebrew.
And just a block away was the synagogue from-- from-- from the public school, from the elementary school.
And we were learning-- we learned Hebrew.
And of course, I read Hebrew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this was not only so for Jewish kids.
There were Mormons.
There was some Mormon kids who were-- who were also excused, and-- and there were some-- there was a Russian school that also taught the Russian language.
So some of the White Russians in the area sent their kids to Russian school.
They-- they did not teach them religion, I don't think-- possibly the Greek Catholic religion, not the Roman Catholic, but the Greek Catholic religion.
There were other-- other schools.
I think there were three schools all together, each taught a-- a different religious-- religion.
Oh, yes, my brothers were well prepared, much more so than the girls.
They-- they studied the Mishnah and the Gemara, and they knew exactly how to-- what every word meant that they read, whereas, I never had that opportunity.
Of course, I was much younger than either of my four brothers were.
So consequently, I missed out on that, but that's something that I can do on my own here.
Well, we-- for most-- for meat, we would have a-- a ritual slaughterer come to our town for cattle, to slaughter the cattle.
However-- and the chickens, the poultry, was also done by a shochet.
A shochet is also a-- a ritual-- ritual slaughterer, but it takes special facilities to, apparently, slaughter a-- a larger animal, such as a cow or a calf.
So there was plenty of kosher food.
Not until later did this become a problem.
After 1938, our shochet was taken away from us, I think, around-- around 1942, approximately.
And we-- we were left without a shochet.
And so we would have to go eight kilometers into Beregszasz to-- to have our chickens slaughtered.
And I used to be sent by my mother, by myself, and on my bicycle, and there was a-- a live chicken in a basket.
[LAUGHS] And I would bicycle into Beregszasz and have the chicken slaughtered on Thursdays, so that on Shabbat-- on Friday, my mother could cook it for Shabbat.
Oh, goodness.
We-- we really sang a lot.
The boys played various games.
Chess was a big, big game among the men.
And I would watch and-- you're too little yet, I was told.
I-- I-- but-- I-- I learned how to move the pieces before they started teaching me.
[LAUGHS] But I stopped playing chess when my children started beating me.
[LAUGHS] They played with walnuts.
[INAUDIBLE] Lots of walnuts grew in our area.
And there was a game-- they would line up the walnuts, and then they would use another walnut to knock off the walnuts.
So usually from right to left, and depending on where-- where that walnut would go through, if it went through, the-- the player would win the walnuts that was cut off to the right.
So my brothers would bring home more walnuts, and mother said, that's all I need is more walnuts.
Here I'm trying to give them away.
There was so much, usually into the next season.
And we did, very often, give away things that we grew and that we had plenty of.
We had fruit orchards, as well, and apples, oranges, pears, and currants.
It was really a very rich area near the Carpathian Mountains, and the dark soil would fertilize it each-- each spring as it washed down into the fields.
Well, we-- we didn't sell them, no.
We-- we-- usually Mother made-- they bottled it.
It's canning here, but we bottled it.
And the-- the plums, for example, would be turned into jams-- wonderful jams.
We had a huge copper kettle, which also went from farm to farm.
We owned it.
And there was a very interesting story behind it, but first allow me to tell you what we did.
It was a 24-hour process to cook in this huge kettle all the plums.
And it was a big party, and the relatives and friends were invited.
Now, the inside of this huge, huge kettle was an agitator with a long handle, and a special side wall was built around it so that it would not move.
And they let it dry.
And-- and there was a fire under it, a very slow fire, and it had to be constantly stirred.
And it was really fun, and we would sing and have a party.
I always looked forward to this.
It's a harvest party, around Sukkot, around the time of the harvesting period.
But when the Hungarians came in, they began to collect copper kettles and pots.
And they went from house to house, in the name of the Hungarian government, and collected all copper utensils, as well.
And they took our kettle, unfortunately.
And the whole town missed it, because there were, I think, only a couple of them in the entire town.
After that, we just had to use pots that-- pots that were not suitable for this purpose.
First of all, it was not big enough.
Life there was really friendly.
We lived together with non-Jewish neighbors and all the Jews.
There was no Jewish section.
We lived together with every type of person, except for the Gypsies.

They had to live in the outer part of town, right by the cemetery-- well, it's the beginning of town if you come from Beregszasz, or it's the end of town, of course, if you come from the other direction.
And they had to-- they were not permitted to live in-- in the city, and they were not permitted to work.
They had a hard time making a living, and they had usually sizable families, and this reduced them to begging.
And the Jewish people who have-- who have this tremendous history of hardships sympathized with them-- that if they are not permitted to own land, how can they be expected to-- to live?
And of course, it was expected of them to move on from town to town.
And of course, the reason they moved on is because they could not put down stakes anywhere, not for long.

There were some families, I remember as long back as I can remember my life, and I remember during harvesting period, they would always come, and they always knew that Dad would share the harvest with them.
They were also good musicians.
This is the one thing that they-- one activity that they were permitted to-- to do.
They dressed in very colorful clothing-- usually long, which at that time was seen only on old people, and old people wore dark clothes or-- with white blouses, or this sort of thing.
But nobody wore colorful clothes as the Gypsies do, and I always loved it on them.
Ruffles were usually everywhere.
And it was very colorful as they sang their songs.
Usually the head of the family played the violin, and the boy children were taught to play the violin.
And the girl children and-- adults and girls were-- dance to this music, very, very colorfully.
They had dark black, curly hair, and usually they wore them in-- in braids, either down or up.
They seemed very good natured.
They, of course, were reduced to stealing because during the winter, they came upon hard times, when-- when they could not go and beg for food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
May I tell you a little bit about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the other activities?
We had concerts in our town, and my brother Sandor was a member of the brass band.
He played the trumpet.
And I joined the choir.
[LAUGHS] And we sang songs, usually folk songs some-- or the national anthem.
So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] usually at these occasions, people would come and-- and sit around in a large area that would fit-- were a lot of people-- that would hold a lot of people.
And we just enjoyed doing this sort of thing.
And also soccer-- the boys, my four brothers, were ardent soccer players.
And this, once in awhile, caused some injuries.
[LAUGHS]
